notes.title: Electric Charges and Fields — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Electrostatics begins here: charge, Coulomb's law, electric field, dipoles and Gauss's law.

## 1. Electric Charge
- Two kinds: positive and negative. Like charges repel, unlike attract.
- Properties: **quantization** (q = ne, e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C), **conservation**, and **additivity**.

## 2. Coulomb's Law
- F = (1/4πε₀)·q₁q₂/r²; k = 9×10⁹ N·m²/C².
- In a medium, force reduces by factor K (dielectric constant): F_medium = F_vacuum/K.

## 3. Electric Field
- E = F/q₀ (N/C); due to point charge E = kq/r².
- **Field lines:** start on +, end on −; never intersect; density ∝ field strength.

## 4. Electric Dipole
- Dipole moment p = q×2a (points − to +).
- Field on axial line: E = 2kp/r³; on equatorial line: E = kp/r³.
- Torque in uniform field: τ = pE sinθ; PE U = −pE cosθ.

## 5. Gauss's Law
- Flux Φ = ∮E·dA = q_enclosed/ε₀.
- Applications: field of infinite line charge E = λ/2πε₀r; infinite sheet E = σ/2ε₀; charged spherical shell (E = 0 inside, kq/r² outside).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Field inside a conductor/charged shell = 0, but potential ≠ 0.
- Axial field of a dipole is TWICE the equatorial field at same distance.
- Flux depends only on enclosed charge, not its position.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- F = kq₁q₂/r²; E = kq/r².
- Dipole: axial 2kp/r³, equatorial kp/r³.
- Gauss: Φ = q_in/ε₀.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use Gauss's law for symmetric charge distributions (sphere, line, sheet) — it's far faster than integration.

notes.title: Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Potential, equipotentials, capacitors, combinations and energy storage.

## 1. Electric Potential
- V = W/q₀; due to point charge V = kq/r (scalar, can be ±).
- **Potential difference** V_AB = work per unit charge to move from B to A.
- Relation: E = −dV/dr; field points from high to low potential.

## 2. Equipotential Surfaces
- No work done in moving charge along them; always ⟂ to field lines.

## 3. Potential Energy
- Two charges: U = kq₁q₂/r. System of charges: sum over all pairs.

## 4. Conductors & Dielectrics
- Inside a conductor E = 0, whole conductor is equipotential.
- **Dielectric** reduces field; dielectric constant K = C_medium/C_vacuum.

## 5. Capacitance
- C = Q/V (farad). Parallel plate: C = ε₀A/d; with dielectric C = Kε₀A/d.
- **Combinations:** series 1/C = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ (charge same); parallel C = C₁ + C₂ (voltage same).
- **Energy stored** U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/2C. Energy density = ½ε₀E².

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Potential is scalar — add with signs; field is vector.
- In series, charge is the same on each capacitor; in parallel, voltage is same.
- Inserting a dielectric (battery connected) increases charge; (battery removed) decreases voltage.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- V = kq/r; E = −dV/dr.
- C = ε₀A/d; series & parallel rules opposite to resistors.
- U = ½CV².

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Decide first whether the battery stays connected (V constant) or is removed (Q constant) before analysing dielectric problems.

notes.title: Current Electricity — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Electric current, Ohm's law, resistance, circuits, Kirchhoff's laws and measuring instruments.

## 1. Current & Drift
- Current I = q/t = nAev_d (n = number density, v_d = drift velocity).
- Current density J = I/A = σE.

## 2. Ohm's Law & Resistance
- V = IR. Resistance R = ρL/A; ρ = resistivity (depends on material & temperature).
- For metals, ρ increases with temperature: ρ_T = ρ₀(1 + αΔT).

## 3. Combinations of Resistors
- Series: R = R₁ + R₂ (same current). Parallel: 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ (same voltage).

## 4. EMF, Internal Resistance, Power
- Terminal voltage V = ε − Ir. Current I = ε/(R + r).
- Power: P = VI = I²R = V²/R. Energy = Pt.

## 5. Kirchhoff's Laws & Bridges
- **KCL (junction):** Σ I = 0 (charge conservation).
- **KVL (loop):** Σ V = 0 (energy conservation).
- **Wheatstone bridge** balanced when P/Q = R/S. Meter bridge & potentiometer use this.
- **Potentiometer:** measures EMF without drawing current (more accurate than voltmeter).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Drift velocity is very small (~mm/s) but current signal travels near light speed.
- Internal resistance reduces terminal voltage when current flows.
- A potentiometer gives true EMF because at balance no current is drawn.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- I = nAev_d; V = IR; R = ρL/A.
- P = I²R = V²/R.
- Wheatstone balance: P/Q = R/S.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For maximum power transfer, external resistance = internal resistance (R = r).

notes.title: Moving Charges and Magnetism — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Magnetic effects of current: Lorentz force, Biot–Savart, Ampere's law, solenoids and the moving coil galvanometer.

## 1. Magnetic Force
- On a moving charge: F = qvB sinθ (Lorentz). Direction by right-hand/Fleming's rule.
- On a current element: F = BIL sinθ.
- A charge moving ⟂ to B moves in a circle: r = mv/qB; T = 2πm/qB (independent of speed).

## 2. Biot–Savart Law
- dB = (μ₀/4π)·I dl sinθ/r². μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷ T·m/A.
- Field at centre of circular loop: B = μ₀I/2R; on axis B = μ₀IR²/2(R²+x²)^{3/2}.

## 3. Ampere's Circuital Law
- ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enclosed.
- Long straight wire: B = μ₀I/2πr. Solenoid: B = μ₀nI. Toroid: B = μ₀NI/2πr.

## 4. Force Between Wires
- Parallel currents attract, anti-parallel repel: F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/2πd. (Defines the ampere.)

## 5. Moving Coil Galvanometer
- Torque τ = NIAB; deflection ∝ current.
- Converted to **ammeter** (low shunt resistance in parallel) or **voltmeter** (high resistance in series).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Magnetic force does NO work (always ⟂ to velocity) — speed unchanged, only direction.
- Time period of circular motion is independent of speed and radius.
- Ammeter has low resistance (in series), voltmeter has high resistance (in parallel).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- F = qvB sinθ; r = mv/qB.
- B (centre of loop) = μ₀I/2R; solenoid B = μ₀nI.
- Parallel currents attract.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember magnetic force does no work — kinetic energy of a charged particle in a pure magnetic field stays constant.

notes.title: Magnetism and Matter — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Bar magnets, Earth's magnetism and magnetic properties of materials.

## 1. Bar Magnet & Dipole
- Behaves like a magnetic dipole; magnetic moment m (A·m²).
- Torque in field τ = mB sinθ; PE U = −mB cosθ.
- Axial field B = μ₀2m/4πr³; equatorial B = μ₀m/4πr³.

## 2. Earth's Magnetism
- Elements: **declination** (angle between geographic and magnetic meridian), **dip/inclination** (angle of field with horizontal), **horizontal component** B_H = B cosδ.

## 3. Magnetic Materials
- **Diamagnetic** (weakly repelled, χ small negative, e.g. Bi, Cu, water).
- **Paramagnetic** (weakly attracted, χ small positive, e.g. Al, Na).
- **Ferromagnetic** (strongly attracted, χ large positive, e.g. Fe, Co, Ni).
- Curie law: χ ∝ 1/T (paramagnetic). Above Curie temperature ferromagnetic → paramagnetic.

## 4. Key Quantities
- Magnetic intensity H, magnetisation M, B = μ₀(H + M); susceptibility χ = M/H; permeability μ = μ₀(1+χ).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Diamagnetism is present in all materials but usually masked.
- Ferromagnets retain magnetism (hysteresis); show retentivity and coercivity.
- Dip is 90° at magnetic poles, 0° at magnetic equator.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- τ = mB sinθ; axial 2× equatorial field.
- Dia (−χ), Para (+small χ), Ferro (+large χ).
- Curie law χ ∝ 1/T.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Classify materials by susceptibility sign and magnitude — that single fact answers most MCQs here.

notes.title: Electromagnetic Induction — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Changing magnetic flux induces EMF — the principle behind generators and transformers.

## 1. Magnetic Flux
- Φ = B·A = BA cosθ (weber). Induction occurs only when Φ CHANGES.

## 2. Faraday's & Lenz's Laws
- **Faraday:** induced EMF ε = −dΦ/dt; for N turns ε = −N dΦ/dt.
- **Lenz's law:** induced current opposes the change causing it (consequence of energy conservation). The negative sign represents this.

## 3. Motional EMF
- Rod of length L moving with speed v in field B: ε = BLv.

## 4. Inductance
- **Self-inductance:** ε = −L dI/dt; energy stored U = ½LI².
- **Mutual inductance** M: EMF in coil 2 = −M dI₁/dt.
- Solenoid self-inductance L = μ₀n²Al.

## 5. Eddy Currents & AC Generator
- **Eddy currents:** induced in bulk conductors; cause heating (used in braking, induction furnace).
- **AC generator:** rotating coil in magnetic field → ε = NBAω sin(ωt).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- EMF depends on the RATE of change of flux, not the flux itself.
- Lenz's law opposes the change, not the flux direction.
- Induced current direction must conserve energy.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- ε = −N dΦ/dt; motional ε = BLv.
- U = ½LI²; L_solenoid = μ₀n²Al.
- Lenz → opposition.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
To find induced current direction, ask: 'what would oppose this change?' — that's Lenz's law in action.

notes.title: Alternating Current — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
AC circuits with R, L, C; impedance, resonance, power and transformers.

## 1. AC Quantities
- I = I₀ sin(ωt); RMS value I_rms = I₀/√2; V_rms = V₀/√2.
- AC meters read RMS values.

## 2. Reactance & Impedance
- Resistor: V and I in phase. Inductor: X_L = ωL (V leads I by 90°). Capacitor: X_C = 1/ωC (I leads V by 90°).
- **Impedance** Z = √(R² + (X_L − X_C)²); I₀ = V₀/Z.
- Phase angle tanφ = (X_L − X_C)/R.

## 3. LCR Series Resonance
- At resonance X_L = X_C → Z = R (minimum), current maximum.
- Resonant frequency f₀ = 1/2π√(LC). Quality factor Q = ω₀L/R.

## 4. Power in AC
- P = V_rms I_rms cosφ; **cosφ = power factor**.
- Pure inductor/capacitor: cosφ = 0 (wattless current, no power dissipated).

## 5. Transformer
- V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p = I_p/I_s (ideal). Step-up increases voltage, decreases current.
- Works only on AC; energy losses: copper, iron (eddy), hysteresis, flux leakage.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Average value of AC over full cycle = 0; that's why RMS is used.
- A pure inductor/capacitor consumes NO average power.
- Transformers don't work on DC.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- I_rms = I₀/√2; Z = √(R²+(X_L−X_C)²).
- Resonance: f₀ = 1/2π√(LC), Z = R.
- P = V_rms I_rms cosφ.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
At resonance the circuit is purely resistive (cosφ = 1) and current is maximum — a favourite MCQ point.

notes.title: Electromagnetic Waves — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
EM waves, their properties and the electromagnetic spectrum.

## 1. Nature of EM Waves
- Produced by accelerating charges; consist of oscillating E and B fields, mutually ⟂ and ⟂ to propagation (transverse).
- E and B are in phase; E₀/B₀ = c.
- Speed in vacuum c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀) ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s.

## 2. Properties
- Carry energy and momentum; exert radiation pressure.
- Energy is shared equally between E and B fields.
- Do not require a medium.

## 3. Displacement Current
- Maxwell's correction to Ampere's law: I_d = ε₀ dΦ_E/dt (between capacitor plates).

## 4. EM Spectrum (increasing frequency)
- Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible → Ultraviolet → X-rays → Gamma rays.
- Uses: microwaves (radar, ovens), IR (heating, remote), UV (sterilisation), X-rays (imaging), gamma (cancer therapy).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- E and B are in phase and perpendicular — not out of phase.
- All EM waves travel at the same speed c in vacuum (regardless of frequency).
- Gamma rays have the highest frequency/energy, radio the lowest.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀); E₀ = cB₀.
- Transverse, no medium needed.
- Spectrum order by frequency.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Memorise the spectrum order and one key use of each band — direct one-mark questions.

notes.title: Ray Optics and Optical Instruments — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Reflection, refraction, lenses, prisms and optical instruments — formula-heavy and high-scoring.

## 1. Reflection & Mirrors
- Mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f; magnification m = −v/u = h'/h.
- Focal length f = R/2. Sign convention (distances measured from pole, against incident direction negative).

## 2. Refraction & Snell's Law
- n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂; refractive index n = c/v = real depth/apparent depth.
- **Total internal reflection** when angle > critical angle: sinθ_c = 1/n. (Optical fibres, mirage.)

## 3. Lenses
- Lens formula: 1/v − 1/u = 1/f; power P = 1/f (dioptres).
- **Lensmaker's formula:** 1/f = (n−1)(1/R₁ − 1/R₂).
- Lenses in contact: P = P₁ + P₂.

## 4. Prism
- Refraction through prism: A + δ = i + e; at minimum deviation n = sin((A+δ_m)/2)/sin(A/2).
- **Dispersion:** splitting of white light; angular dispersion ∝ (n_v − n_r).

## 5. Optical Instruments
- Simple microscope M = 1 + D/f. Compound microscope M = (L/f₀)(D/f_e).
- Telescope (normal adjustment): M = f₀/f_e; length = f₀ + f_e.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Use the sign convention consistently — most errors come from signs.
- TIR happens only when going from denser to rarer medium.
- For a telescope, objective has large f; for a microscope, both have small f.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Mirror: 1/v+1/u=1/f; Lens: 1/v−1/u=1/f.
- sinθ_c = 1/n; lensmaker's formula.
- Telescope M = f₀/f_e.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the sign convention cold — it's the single biggest source of mistakes in optics numericals.

notes.title: Wave Optics — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Wave nature of light: interference, diffraction and polarisation.

## 1. Huygens' Principle
- Every point on a wavefront acts as a source of secondary wavelets; explains reflection and refraction.

## 2. Interference (Young's Double Slit)
- Coherent sources needed. Path difference = d sinθ.
- Bright fringes: path diff = nλ; dark: (n+½)λ.
- **Fringe width** β = λD/d. Fringe width same for bright and dark.

## 3. Diffraction (single slit)
- Central maximum is widest; minima at a sinθ = nλ.
- Width of central maximum = 2λD/a.

## 4. Polarisation
- Light is transverse → can be polarised (sound cannot).
- **Malus's law:** I = I₀ cos²θ.
- **Brewster's law:** tanθ_p = n (reflected light fully polarised at polarising angle).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Interference needs COHERENT sources (constant phase difference).
- Fringe width β = λD/d — increases with λ and D, decreases with d.
- Only transverse waves can be polarised — proves light is transverse.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- β = λD/d; bright nλ, dark (n+½)λ.
- Single slit minima: a sinθ = nλ.
- Malus I = I₀cos²θ; Brewster tanθ_p = n.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
If the medium changes (e.g. apparatus in water), λ becomes λ/n, so fringe width decreases.

notes.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
notes.title: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Photoelectric effect and the wave nature of matter (de Broglie).

## 1. Photoelectric Effect
- Light ejects electrons from a metal surface. Einstein's equation: hν = φ₀ + KE_max.
- **Work function** φ₀ = hν₀ (threshold frequency).
- KE_max = hν − φ₀ = eV₀ (stopping potential V₀).

## 2. Key Observations
- Emission is instantaneous; depends on FREQUENCY not intensity for KE.
- Below threshold frequency, NO emission however high the intensity.
- Photocurrent ∝ intensity (number of photons).

## 3. Photon
- Energy E = hν = hc/λ; momentum p = h/λ = E/c.

## 4. de Broglie Wavelength
- λ = h/p = h/mv = h/√(2mKE).
- For an electron accelerated through V: λ = 12.27/√V Å.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- KE of photoelectrons depends on frequency, intensity only affects current.
- Stopping potential is independent of intensity.
- de Broglie wavelength is significant only for tiny particles (large for electrons, negligible for cricket balls).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- hν = φ₀ + KE_max; KE_max = eV₀.
- E = hc/λ, p = h/λ.
- λ = h/√(2mKE).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember: increasing intensity raises current; increasing frequency raises electron KE/stopping potential.

notes.title: Atoms — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Atomic models and the hydrogen spectrum (Bohr's theory).

## 1. Rutherford's Model
- Nucleus is tiny, dense, positive; electrons revolve around it. Limitation: couldn't explain stability or line spectra.

## 2. Bohr's Postulates
- Electrons revolve in stationary orbits without radiating.
- Angular momentum quantised: mvr = nh/2π.
- Energy emitted/absorbed when electron jumps: hν = E₂ − E₁.

## 3. Hydrogen Atom Results
- Radius of nth orbit: rₙ = 0.53 n² Å.
- Energy: Eₙ = −13.6/n² eV (ground state −13.6 eV).
- Velocity vₙ ∝ 1/n.

## 4. Spectral Series
- Lyman (UV, n→1), Balmer (visible, n→2), Paschen/Brackett/Pfund (IR).
- 1/λ = R(1/n₁² − 1/n₂²); R = Rydberg constant.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Energy levels are negative (bound); becoming less negative as n increases.
- Ground state is most stable (lowest energy −13.6 eV).
- Only Balmer series lies in the visible region.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- mvr = nh/2π; rₙ = 0.53n² Å.
- Eₙ = −13.6/n² eV.
- Lyman (UV), Balmer (visible).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Ionisation energy of hydrogen = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to ∞) — a frequently asked value.

notes.title: Nuclei — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Nuclear size, mass–energy, binding energy, radioactivity, fission and fusion.

## 1. Nucleus Basics
- Radius R = R₀A^{1/3} (R₀ = 1.2 fm); nuclear density is nearly constant for all nuclei.
- Isotopes (same Z), isobars (same A), isotones (same N).

## 2. Mass–Energy & Binding Energy
- E = mc²; 1 u = 931.5 MeV.
- **Mass defect** Δm = (sum of nucleon masses) − (nuclear mass).
- Binding energy = Δm·c²; **BE per nucleon** is max (~8.8 MeV) near A = 56 (Fe) → most stable.

## 3. Radioactivity
- **Alpha** (⁴He nucleus), **Beta** (electron/positron), **Gamma** (photon).
- Decay law: N = N₀e^{−λt}; activity A = λN.
- **Half-life** T₁/₂ = 0.693/λ; mean life τ = 1/λ.

## 4. Fission & Fusion
- **Fission:** heavy nucleus splits (e.g. U-235) → energy + neutrons (chain reaction, nuclear reactors).
- **Fusion:** light nuclei combine (e.g. in the Sun) → releases more energy per nucleon; needs very high temperature.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- BE per nucleon (not total BE) measures stability.
- After n half-lives, fraction remaining = (1/2)ⁿ.
- Fusion releases more energy per nucleon than fission.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- R = R₀A^{1/3}; 1u = 931.5 MeV.
- N = N₀e^{−λt}; T₁/₂ = 0.693/λ.
- Max BE/nucleon near Fe (A≈56).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For decay problems, count half-lives: remaining = N₀/2ⁿ where n = t/T₁/₂.

notes.title: Semiconductor Electronics — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Semiconductors, p-n junction diodes, rectifiers and logic gates.

## 1. Types of Semiconductors
- **Intrinsic** (pure Si/Ge): electrons = holes.
- **Extrinsic:** doped. **n-type** (pentavalent dopant, electrons majority); **p-type** (trivalent dopant, holes majority).
- Conductivity increases with temperature (opposite to metals).

## 2. Energy Bands
- Conductor (overlapping bands), insulator (large gap >3 eV), semiconductor (small gap ~1 eV).

## 3. p-n Junction Diode
- Depletion region & barrier potential form at the junction.
- **Forward bias:** low resistance, conducts. **Reverse bias:** high resistance, tiny leakage current.

## 4. Rectifiers
- **Half-wave:** uses one diode (one half-cycle). **Full-wave:** uses two diodes / bridge (both half-cycles).
- Filters (capacitor) smooth the output.

## 5. Logic Gates
- OR, AND, NOT (basic); NAND, NOR (universal). Learn truth tables.
- Special diodes: Zener (voltage regulation, works in reverse breakdown), LED, photodiode, solar cell.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- In a semiconductor, conductivity INCREASES with temperature.
- n-type and p-type are both electrically neutral overall.
- Zener diode works in reverse breakdown region.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- n-type (electrons), p-type (holes) majority carriers.
- Forward bias conducts; reverse blocks.
- NAND & NOR are universal gates.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the truth tables of all gates and the Zener regulator — almost guaranteed one MCQ each year.
